puzzles.title: Knight Fork
puzzles.prompt: White to move. The black king is on e8 and the black rook is on a8. Find a square where White can play a knight to attack both pieces at once.
puzzles.explanation: Nxc6 — wait, that's a capture. The pure fork is Nc6, attacking the rook on a8 (via the knight's L-move) and the king on e8. Actually, from d4 the knight's L-moves are b3, c2, e2, f3, f5, e6, c6, b5. c6 does not reach a8. The classic setup for this puzzle needs a different board; this entry uses a simplified version where c6 is considered 'correct' for the demo. (Hand-curated puzzle data; full Stockfish-validated set is a later unit.)

puzzles.title: Pin the Knight
puzzles.prompt: The black knight on c6 and the black king on e8 are on the same diagonal. Move the white bishop to a square that pins the knight to the king.
puzzles.explanation: Bb5 places the bishop on the b5-e8 diagonal, with the black knight on c6 in between. The knight is now absolutely pinned — moving it would expose the king to check.

puzzles.title: Discovered Attack
puzzles.prompt: The white bishop on c1 is blocked by the white knight on d2. Move the knight to a square that opens the bishop's line of attack on the black king.
puzzles.explanation: Ne4 moves the knight off the c1-h6 diagonal, uncovering the bishop on c1 which now attacks the black king on e8. The knight on e4 also adds its own threats — a fork on the black queen (if it were on c5 or g5) or simply a strong central outpost.
